-{"id":"bd-1e2if.1","title":"[METHODOLOGY] Capture baseline p50/p95/p99 for all primary operations","description":"Run comprehensive benchmarks for all primary Asupersync operations and capture baseline performance metrics. This is the foundation for all future optimization work -- without baselines, we cannot measure improvement or detect regressions.

BACKGROUND (section 0.11 Tail-Latency Decomposition): The Alien CS Graveyard methodology requires statistical benchmarking with p50/p95/p99 percentiles and bootstrap confidence intervals. Tail-latency decomposition identifies which subsystem contributes most to high-percentile latency. For Asupersync, the tail terms that usually dominate are: queueing (scheduler queue depth), synchronization (lock contention), and retries (cancellation retry count).

OPERATIONS TO BENCHMARK: (1) Task spawn -- measure time from spawn call to first poll. (2) Task cancellation -- measure time from cancel signal to all obligations released. (3) Channel send/recv -- measure one-way latency for both bounded and unbounded channels. (4) Cx capability check -- measure has<T>() and get<T>() latency. (5) Budget check -- measure budget remaining check and propagation latency. (6) RaptorQ encode/decode -- measure encoding and decoding throughput at various block sizes. (7) DPOR exploration -- measure exploration rate (states/second) for a standard test program.

RUST IMPLEMENTATION GUIDANCE: (1) Use criterion.rs with sample_size(100) and measurement_time(Duration::from_secs(10)) for stable results. (2) Each benchmark produces: p50, p95, p99 latencies and 95% bootstrap confidence intervals. (3) Store results in artifacts/baseline.json with schema: { operation: string, p50_ns: u64, p95_ns: u64, p99_ns: u64, ci95_lower_ns: u64, ci95_upper_ns: u64, timestamp: string, git_sha: string }. (4) First mandatory counters for tail decomposition: scheduler_queue_depth (histogram), cancellation_retry_count (counter), wake_to_run_latency_us (histogram). Expose via metrics crate with prometheus exporter. (5) Use black_box() to prevent compiler optimization of benchmark targets. (6) Benchmark both cold (first run) and warm (steady state) performance.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA: (1) Baselines captured for all 7 operations listed above. (2) Each baseline has valid confidence intervals (CI width < 20% of median). (3) Results are reproducible: running baseline twice produces overlapping CIs. (4) artifacts/baseline.json is committed to the repository with git SHA tag. (5) CI can compare new results against baseline and flag >5% regression. (6) Tail-latency counters (queue depth, retry count, wake-to-run) are operational and emitting.

LOGGING REQUIREMENTS: Each benchmark run logs: operation_name, sample_count, p50_ns, p95_ns, p99_ns, ci95_lower_ns, ci95_upper_ns, git_sha, timestamp, machine_info (cpu, os). Tail-latency counters are emitted as prometheus-style metrics: asupersync_scheduler_queue_depth_histogram, asupersync_cancellation_retry_count_total, asupersync_wake_to_run_latency_us_histogram. Summary report generated as Markdown for PR comments.

TESTING APPROACH: (1) Verify benchmark harness itself: a known-cost operation (sleep 1ms) should benchmark at ~1ms +/- 10%. (2) Verify baseline stability: run same benchmark 5 times, assert all p50 values are within 10% of each other. (3) Verify regression detection: artificially degrade an operation by 10%, assert CI pipeline flags it.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:18:32.998835007Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T18:49:48.244540585Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["baseline","benchmarks","methodology"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1e2if.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1e2if","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:30:42Z","created_by":"import"}],"comments":[{"id":1397,"issue_id":"bd-1e2if.1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Methodology baselines benchmark suite created (benches/methodology_baselines.rs). 46 benchmarks across 5 groups: task spawn (6), task cancellation (5), channel send/recv (6), Cx capability (11), budget check (18). Operations 6-7 (RaptorQ/DPOR) already covered by existing suites. Generated artifacts/baseline.json. All gates pass.","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:49:48Z"}]}
-{"id":"bd-1e2if.2","title":"[METHODOLOGY] Add golden output checksums for core test suite","description":"Implement golden output checksums for Asupersync's core test suite. Golden checksums catch silent semantic changes that pass assertions but produce different outputs -- a critical gap in traditional testing.

BACKGROUND (section 0 Methodology): The Alien CS Graveyard methodology requires golden output checksums as one of the four CI artifact gates (alongside baseline benchmarks, flamegraphs, and proof notes). Golden checksums provide a deterministic fingerprint of system behavior that detects any change in observable output, even if all assertions still pass.

RUST IMPLEMENTATION GUIDANCE: (1) Define a GoldenChecksum struct: { test_name: String, output_hash: String (SHA-256 hex), schema_version: u32, generated_at: String, git_sha: String }. (2) Store checksums in artifacts/golden_checksums.json as a sorted array (stable for git diff). (3) Use sha2 crate for SHA-256 hashing. (4) Capture mechanism: each test that opts into golden checksums calls golden::capture(test_name, &output) which hashes the output and records it. (5) Verification: in CI, golden::verify(test_name, &output) compares against stored checksum and fails with a clear diff if mismatched. (6) Update workflow: GOLDEN_UPDATE=1 cargo test regenerates all checksums. Changed checksums require a justification comment in the PR. (7) Integration with evidence ledger: each checksum change is logged as an EvidenceLedger entry with component=golden_checksums, action=checksum_changed|checksum_verified.

COVERAGE: All unit tests for: obligation system (SendPermit lifecycle, Lease lifecycle, Reserve/Commit lifecycle), channel operations (send/recv ordering, backpressure behavior), scheduler (task ordering under various policies), cancellation protocol (cancel propagation, obligation cleanup), budget propagation (tropical semiring arithmetic), and Lab runtime replay (deterministic scheduling output).

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA: (1) Golden checksums exist for all core test categories listed above. (2) CI fails if any test output diverges from golden checksum (exit code 1 with clear error message). (3) GOLDEN_UPDATE=1 regenerates checksums and outputs a diff summary. (4) Checksum stability: running the same test 100 times produces the same checksum (determinism requirement). (5) Schema version in checksum file enables forward-compatible migration when test output format changes.

LOGGING REQUIREMENTS: Each golden checksum verification logs: test_name, expected_hash, actual_hash, match (bool), verification_time_us. On mismatch: log expected vs actual hash, output size, and first differing byte position. Summary at CI completion: total_verified, passed, failed, skipped. All logs in structured JSON for CI parsing. EvidenceLedger entry for each checksum change with justification field.

TESTING APPROACH: (1) Meta-test: verify the golden checksum framework itself -- capture a checksum, modify the output, verify detection. (2) Stability test: run all golden tests 10 times, verify all produce identical checksums. (3) Update workflow test: verify GOLDEN_UPDATE=1 correctly regenerates and the diff is human-readable.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:18:40.821017062Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T18:59:29.335400578Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["golden-checksums","methodology","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1e2if.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1e2if","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:30:15Z","created_by":"import"}],"comments":[{"id":1398,"issue_id":"bd-1e2if.2","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Golden checksum JSON artifact and GOLDEN_UPDATE workflow landed (ac61bcc). 14 checksums across 6 subsystems in artifacts/golden_checksums.json. 4 new scenarios: budget combine_chain, deadline_check_matrix, obligation send_permit_lifecycle, region_cancel_propagation. GOLDEN_UPDATE=1 workflow for regeneration. All gates pass.","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:59:29Z"}]}

 {"id":"bd-1e2if.5","title":"[METHODOLOGY] Set up CI artifact gates (baseline, flamegraph, golden checksums, proof note)","description":"Set up CI artifact gates that block merges unless all required artifacts are present and valid. This enforces the Alien CS Graveyard section 0 methodology at the CI level -- no PR can merge without producing the required evidence artifacts.

BACKGROUND (section 0 CI Gates): The methodology requires four mandatory artifacts per PR: (1) Baseline benchmark comparison, (2) Flamegraph for changed hot paths, (3) Golden checksum validation, (4) Proof note for safety-critical changes. These gates prevent regression without manual review overhead.

RUST IMPLEMENTATION GUIDANCE: (1) GitHub Actions workflow with four separate jobs, one per gate. (2) Benchmark gate: run criterion.rs benchmarks, use critcmp or criterion-compare to diff against main branch baselines in artifacts/baseline.json. Fail if any p99 metric regresses by >5% with statistical significance (non-overlapping 95% CIs). (3) Flamegraph gate: detect changed files in hot-path modules (src/scheduler/, src/channel/, src/obligation/). If changed, require a flamegraph artifact in artifacts/flamegraphs/<pr_id>.svg. Generate with cargo flamegraph --freq 997 (prime frequency avoids aliasing). (4) Golden checksum gate: run all golden-checksummed tests and verify against artifacts/golden_checksums.json. Any mismatch fails the gate. (5) Proof note gate: if the PR touches files matching src/obligation/*, src/safety/*, or any file with unsafe blocks, require artifacts/proof_notes/<pr_id>.md with a formal argument for correctness. (6) Summary comment: post a PR comment with pass/fail status for each gate, benchmark comparison table, and links to artifacts.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA: (1) All four gates run on every PR. (2) Gates block merge when requirements are not met (required status checks). (3) Clear, actionable error messages when a gate fails (what failed, how to fix). (4) Flamegraph gate correctly identifies hot-path changes via file path matching. (5) Proof note gate does not trigger for non-safety-critical changes. (6) Summary comment is posted within 2 minutes of CI completion. (7) Gate configuration is version-controlled (not in GitHub UI).

LOGGING REQUIREMENTS: Each gate logs: gate_name, status (pass|fail|skip), duration_ms, artifact_path, failure_reason (if applicable). Summary log: total_gates, passed, failed, skipped, total_ci_time_ms. PR comment includes: benchmark comparison table (operation, main_p99, pr_p99, delta_pct, status), golden checksum summary (total, passed, failed), proof note status (required/provided/not_required). All structured as JSON for CI dashboard ingestion.

 {"id":"bd-1hu19.5","title":"[WEDGE] Unit and E2E tests for FrankenLab runtime and CLI","description":"Comprehensive test suite for the FrankenLab standalone testing harness, covering the deterministic scheduler, virtual time, scenario YAML format, and CLI.

**Unit tests:**
(1) Deterministic scheduler reproducibility — run the same program with the same seed 100 times; verify bit-identical scheduling decisions each time.
(2) PRNG scheduling strategies — verify that random, round-robin, DPOR, and PCT strategies each produce valid (non-deadlocking) schedules for a standard test program.
(3) Virtual time correctness — verify that sleep(Duration::from_secs(N)) with virtual time completes instantly in wall-clock time but advances virtual clock by exactly N seconds.
(4) Auto-advance semantics — when all tasks block on sleep, virtual time advances to the minimum next wake-up point (not further).
(5) YAML scenario parser — roundtrip property: parse(serialize(scenario)) == scenario for randomly generated scenarios (proptest).
(6) JSON Schema validation — verify that all example scenarios pass schema validation and that intentionally invalid scenarios are rejected with clear errors.
(7) Fault injection determinism — the same fault schedule with the same seed produces identical fault timing across runs.

**E2E tests:**
(8) Full scenario execution — run all 10+ example scenarios through the CLI and verify all assertions pass.
(9) Record/replay round-trip — record an execution trace, replay it, and verify bit-identical outcomes.
(10) Cross-machine reproducibility — generate artifact hashes on CI and verify they match expected golden values (proves determinism is not hardware-dependent).
(11) CLI error handling — verify that malformed YAML, missing files, and invalid seeds produce clear, actionable error messages with non-zero exit codes.
(12) Performance regression — FrankenLab overhead vs direct execution must stay below 10% for small tests (criterion.rs benchmark).

**Logging requirements:**
- Each test must log: scenario_name, seed, strategy, virtual_time_final, wall_time_ms, assertion_results, fault_count.
- On replay divergence, log the first diverging scheduling decision with full context.
- All logs in structured JSON to EvidenceLedger.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- 100% deterministic replay across 1000 runs.
- All example scenarios pass.
- Overhead below 10% for small tests, below 5x for large tests.
- All CLI error cases produce exit code != 0 with human-readable message.

**Rust implementation notes:**
- Use proptest for YAML schema fuzzing.
- Use assert_cmd + predicates for CLI integration tests.
- Use tempfile for isolated test directories.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:21:32.345542617Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T17:22:38.411148668Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["frankenlab","testing","wedge"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1hu19.5","depends_on_id":"bd-1hu19","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:30:14Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-1hu19.5","depends_on_id":"bd-1hu19.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:30:14Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-1hu19.5","depends_on_id":"bd-1hu19.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:30:14Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-1hu19.5","depends_on_id":"bd-1hu19.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:30:14Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-1hu19.5","depends_on_id":"bd-1hu19.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:30:14Z","created_by":"import"}]}

-{"id":"bd-qaaxt.4","title":"[FOUNDATION] Unit and integration tests for franken_evidence crate","description":"Comprehensive test suite for the franken_evidence crate (EvidenceLedger struct, JSONL exporter, galaxy-brain renderer).

**Unit tests:**
(1) EvidenceLedger struct validation — posterior must sum to ~1.0 (within 1e-6 epsilon), calibration_score in [0,1], expected losses non-negative. Use proptest to generate random entries and verify validation catches all invalid states.
(2) Serde roundtrip — serialize to JSON and deserialize back; verify equality for all field types including Vec<f64> posteriors and HashMap<String, f64> loss maps.
(3) Builder pattern ergonomics — verify that the builder produces valid entries and that required fields cause compile errors when omitted (trybuild).
(4) JSONL exporter append-only semantics — write N entries, verify file has exactly N lines, each parseable as a valid EvidenceLedger entry.
(5) JSONL exporter crash recovery — write N entries, truncate the file mid-line, verify the reader skips the partial last line and recovers all N-1 complete entries.
(6) JSONL rotation — write entries until rotation threshold (100MB or 1 hour), verify new file created with schema version header, old file correctly closed.
(7) Zstd compression — verify that compressed rotated files decompress to identical content as uncompressed originals.
(8) Concurrent writer safety — spawn 10 threads writing simultaneously; verify no interleaved lines, no data corruption, all entries recoverable.
(9) Galaxy-brain Level 0 — verify one-liner format contains action, expected loss, and calibration score.
(10) Galaxy-brain Level 1 — verify paragraph format contains all summary fields in human-readable layout.
(11) Galaxy-brain Level 2 — verify ASCII histogram is generated for posterior distribution with correct bin widths.
(12) Galaxy-brain Level 3 — verify diff output when given two consecutive entries from the same component.

**Integration tests:**
(13) Full pipeline — create entries via builder, write to JSONL, read back, render at all 4 galaxy-brain levels; verify end-to-end integrity.
(14) Schema version migration — create a JSONL file with v1 schema, read with v2 schema reader; verify graceful handling (migration or clear error).

**Logging requirements:**
- Each test logs: test_name, entry_count, file_size_bytes, write_latency_p99_us, read_latency_p99_us.
- On corruption detection, log byte offset and hex dump of corrupted region.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Serde roundtrip holds for 10,000 random entries.
- Crash recovery loses at most 1 entry (the partial last line).
- Concurrent writes produce zero corruption across 100 runs with 10 threads each.

 {"id":"bd-qctz","title":"'Explain why' diagnostics: intelligent troubleshooting for runtime issues","description":"# 'Explain Why' Diagnostics Engine

## Overview
Intelligent diagnostic engine that answers questions like 'why can't this region
close?' or 'what's blocking this task?' with detailed, actionable explanations.

## Design

### Diagnostic Queries
```rust
pub struct Diagnostics {
    state: Arc<RuntimeState>,
}

impl Diagnostics {
    /// Why can't this region close?
    pub fn explain_region_open(&self, region_id: RegionId) -> RegionOpenExplanation {
        let region = self.state.get_region(region_id);
        
        let mut reasons = Vec::new();
        
        // Check for running children
        for child in region.children() {
            if \!child.is_closed() {
                reasons.push(Reason::ChildRegionOpen {
                    child_id: child.id(),
                    child_state: child.state(),
                    explanation: self.explain_region_open(child.id()),
                });
            }
        }
        
        // Check for running tasks
        for task in region.tasks() {
            if \!task.is_complete() {
                reasons.push(Reason::TaskRunning {
                    task_id: task.id(),
                    task_state: task.state(),
                    last_checkpoint: task.last_checkpoint_msg(),
                });
            }
        }
        
        // Check for held obligations
        for obligation in region.obligations() {
            if \!obligation.is_fulfilled() {
                reasons.push(Reason::ObligationHeld {
                    obligation_type: obligation.type_name(),
                    held_since: obligation.created_at(),
                    holder: obligation.holder_info(),
                });
            }
        }
        
        RegionOpenExplanation { region_id, reasons }
    }
    
    /// What's blocking this task?
    pub fn explain_task_blocked(&self, task_id: TaskId) -> TaskBlockedExplanation {
        let task = self.state.get_task(task_id);
        
        match task.block_reason() {
            BlockReason::Awaiting(future_info) => {
                TaskBlockedExplanation::AwaitingFuture {
                    future_type: future_info.type_name(),
                    await_location: future_info.location(),
                    duration: future_info.duration(),
                }
            }
            BlockReason::WaitingForLock(lock_info) => {
                TaskBlockedExplanation::WaitingForLock {
                    lock_type: lock_info.type_name(),
                    held_by: lock_info.holder(),
                    wait_duration: lock_info.wait_duration(),
                }
            }
            BlockReason::ChannelFull(channel_info) => {
                TaskBlockedExplanation::ChannelBackpressure {
                    channel: channel_info.name(),
                    capacity: channel_info.capacity(),
                    pending: channel_info.pending_count(),
                }
            }
            // ... other cases
        }
    }
    
    /// Why was this task cancelled?
    pub fn explain_task_cancelled(&self, task_id: TaskId) -> CancellationExplanation {
        let task = self.state.get_task(task_id);
        let cancel_event = task.cancellation_event();
        
        CancellationExplanation {
            task_id,
            cancelled_at: cancel_event.timestamp(),
            source: cancel_event.source(),
            propagation_path: self.trace_cancellation_path(cancel_event),
        }
    }
    
    /// Which obligation is leaking?
    pub fn find_leaked_obligations(&self) -> Vec<ObligationLeak> {
        self.state.all_obligations()
            .filter(|o| o.is_potentially_leaked())
            .map(|o| ObligationLeak {
                obligation_type: o.type_name(),
                created_at: o.created_at(),
                holder_region: o.holder_region(),
                last_activity: o.last_activity(),
                recommendation: self.recommend_fix(&o),
            })
            .collect()
    }
}
```

### Explanation Format
```rust
pub struct RegionOpenExplanation {
    pub region_id: RegionId,
    pub reasons: Vec<Reason>,
}

impl Display for RegionOpenExplanation {
    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut Formatter) -> fmt::Result {
        writeln\!(f, "Region {} cannot close because:", self.region_id)?;
        for (i, reason) in self.reasons.iter().enumerate() {
            writeln\!(f, "  {}. {}", i + 1, reason)?;
        }
        Ok(())
    }
}
```

Actions to resolve:
